title: French dip
subtitle: Thinly sliced beef on a baguette with a hot au jus for dipping
excerpt: Thin-shaved roast beef sirloin piled onto a baguette with nothing but a little mustard, served alongside a cup of hot beef jus to dip the sandwich into. The dipping is the whole point: it softens the bread, warms the beef, and turns a good sandwich into something else entirely.
sourceNotes: The French dip was invented (with contested origin) in Los Angeles in the early twentieth century, attributed to either Philippe's (c.1918) or Cole's (c.1908). Both claim the method of dipping a roast-beef sandwich in the pan drippings was either accidental or intentional, but both agree the sandwich and the jus together are the dish. It became a fixture of American diner culture throughout the mid-twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The beef can be roasted a day ahead and sliced cold; warm the jus and the sliced beef separately when serving.

content.text: The French dip works because the bread softens where it meets the hot jus without going fully wet: you dip the corner of the sandwich, take a bite, and the outer inch of the bread has absorbed just enough liquid to carry the beefy flavour all the way through. The beef should be sliced very thin — a mandoline or a sharp carving knife drawn through a well-rested roast produces the right texture. Thick-cut beef turns the sandwich into a roast-dinner roll, which is a different thing.

The au jus is made from the roasting pan drippings, a splash of wine, and beef stock. Season it carefully: the bread and beef add almost no salt, so the jus carries the seasoning for the whole dish. The baguette should be fresh — a day-old baguette will absorb the jus too fast.

items.prepNote: as a joint, tied if needed

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Season the {{beef-sirloin}} joint generously on all sides with salt and black pepper. Place in the roasting pan and roast for 45 to 55 minutes for medium-rare (internal temperature 57°C), or until it reaches your preferred doneness. Remove and rest on a board for at least 15 minutes before slicing.

Set the roasting pan on the hob over medium heat. Pour in the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock and the {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce. Add the thyme sprigs. Bring to a simmer, scraping up any caramelised bits from the pan. Simmer for 10 minutes until slightly reduced. Strain through a fine sieve into a saucepan, taste, and season with salt and black pepper. Keep warm over very low heat.

Slice the rested beef as thinly as possible (2 to 3 mm) against the grain. Spread {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard on the cut face of each baguette half. Pile the beef generously onto the bottom half. Place the top on. Pour the hot jus into cups alongside. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Au jus is watery and bland
items.cause: Not enough reduction, or pan drippings too sparse
items.fix: Simmer the jus for longer (up to 15 minutes) to concentrate it; if the roasting pan produced few drippings, add a splash of Worcestershire sauce and taste — it adds depth quickly

items.cause: Overcooked beyond medium, or not rested before slicing
items.fix: Rest the joint for at least 15 minutes under foil; the internal temperature will rise several degrees and the juices will redistribute

items.cause: Bread left in the jus too long, or jus too hot when served
items.fix: Serve the jus at a brisk simmer, not a boil; dip for 2 to 3 seconds only — the bread should soften at the edges, not saturate

items.cause: Beef not generously seasoned before roasting
items.fix: Season the joint on all sides at least 30 minutes before roasting; the salt draws into the surface and produces a better crust

content.text: The French dip belongs to Los Angeles, not France. Both Philippe's on North Alameda Street (opened 1908) and Cole's P.E. Buffet on East Sixth Street (also 1908) claim to have invented it, and both are still operating. The name likely comes from the French bread roll rather than any French culinary tradition. The dish grew from the beef roasting culture of American diners in the early twentieth century, where the drippings were too good to discard and the bread was too good to serve dry. Dipping a sandwich into a cup of hot jus remains one of the most satisfying things you can do with leftover roast beef.
